# **BONUS CONTENT**

### **Escape the 9-5: Funding Your Dream Sabbatical Without Going Broke**

In a world obsessed with hustle culture, the idea of stepping away from work for six months—or even a full year—sounds like financial suicide. Yet a sabbatical, once reserved for tenured professors, is becoming the ultimate wealth-building reset for ambitious professionals. The key isn’t quitting impulsively; it’s treating time off as a calculated investment in your most valuable asset: yourself.

Start by reframing the sabbatical as a “mini-retirement.” Financial independence gurus like Vicki Robin and Mr. Money Mustache popularized this concept decades ago, but most people still underestimate the math. A realistic sabbatical fund needs 12–18 months of core living expenses (not your full salary) plus a 20% buffer for surprises. Track every dollar for three months using a simple spreadsheet. Most people discover they can slash expenses 30% without pain—canceling unused subscriptions, negotiating rent, or moving to a lower-cost neighborhood for the final six months of saving.

Next, weaponize your current income. Max out tax-advantaged accounts before you leave so the money keeps growing. If your employer offers a 401(k) match, treat it like free money you’ll never see again once you resign. Build a separate “sabbatical bucket” in a high-yield savings account or short-term bond ladder yielding 4–5%. Automate transfers the day after payday so the money disappears before lifestyle creep can claim it.

Side income streams are sabbatical rocket fuel. Many professionals quietly develop a “bridge business” during evenings—freelance consulting in their industry, digital products, or even renting out a spare room. One software engineer I know automated a niche Notion template business that now pays $2,000 monthly on autopilot. The goal isn’t millions; it’s covering health insurance and travel costs while you recharge.

Health insurance remains the biggest landmine. In the U.S., COBRA can cost $600–800 monthly. Shop short-term plans or international travel insurance if you’re leaving the country. Countries like Portugal or Thailand offer residency visas with low-cost public healthcare, turning your sabbatical into geoarbitrage.

The hidden payoff is career velocity. Studies from the Society for Human Resource Management show that professionals who take intentional breaks return with higher salaries and promotions because they come back rested, networked, and clear-headed. Companies now compete for talent who demonstrate work-life integration, not just workaholism.

A sabbatical isn’t a vacation—it’s a strategic pivot. Calculate your number, protect your health coverage, and build parallel income. When the day arrives to hand in your notice, you won’t be broke. You’ll be free.

body:
MORNING

Apr 07, 2026 5:21:58 AM EST

Vital Dawn for Tuesday April 7, 2026

Morning update

• Market levels – the US equity futures are flat-to-up small so far this morning: S&P +3.2 points/5bp, Dow +62 points/13bp, Nasdaq flat, and R2 K flat. The major Eurozone indices are rallying 65-75bp, and the gains are broad. Areas of outperformance include luxury, basic resources, chemicals, staples, banks, insurance, media, retail, and telecoms while industrials, tech, real estate, healthcare, energy, and utilities underperform . UMG is the top stock in the SXXP on back of the Bill Ackman bid (other top names include WPP, STM, Norsk Hydro, and Yara ). ASML is sliding on the US bill (introduced Thurs night) imposing fresh restrictions on semi equipment sales to China. Stocks in Asia generally saw gains on Tuesday: TPX +0.25%, NKY +0.03%, SHCOMP +0.26%, Shenzhen +0.8%, Taiwan TAIEX +2.02%, Korea KOSPI +0.82%, Australia ASX 200 +1.74%, and India is up ~15bp. Hong Kong was closed. Samsung Electronics opened higher on back of its blow-out Q1 results, but the stock wound up fading, finishing down ~70bp. Treasuries aren’t doing much (yields are flat-to-down 1bp), and Fed expectations are fairly steady too. The DXY is sliding 20bp. Brent is ticking down 35bp (to $109.40). Gold is rallying 90bp while silver ticks up 20bp. Bitcoin is dipping 100bp (to ~$69 K).
• What’s happening this morning – it was fairly quiet on the Iran news front since the US close, although a WSJ article (which was published around 9pm ET Mon night) is receiving some attention. The WSJ report claimed that hopes are “fading” that a final deal can be reached by tonight’s 8pm ET deadline. We agree with that sentiment – a full and final agreement won’t be in place by tonight . However, since the options facing Trump are so bad , since the US has achieved the bulk of its strategic objectives , since negotiations are still ongoing , and since Iran has made some (small) concessions on Hormuz (the Iraq news from the weekend was meaningful), it’s likely that the Pentagon and IDF don’t commence a scorched search campaign against civilian infrastructure targets later today . Away from geopolitics, there were a few company/sector-specific developments out since the Mon close, including blow-out preliminary Q1 results from Samsung , a new TPU agreement between Broadcom and Google (and between Broadcom, Google, and Anthropic), favorable Medicare news for health insurers , and a Bill Ackman takeout bid for Universal Music Group .
• What we’ll be watching on Tuesday – the calendar is fairly sparse , although LEVI will report earnings after the close and Trump’s latest Iran deadline is 8pm ET (Trump on Mon insisted he won’t change the 4/7 deadline , but it could easily shift once again). In addition, a special House election in Georgia (for Marjorie Taylor Greene’s old seat) will be watched for insight into the Nov mid-terms (the Republican is widely expected to win, but a narrow margin of victory could point to trouble for the GOP later in the year).
• What we’ll be watching on Wednesday – the focus will be on Delta’s Q1 earnings report before the open, the FOMC minutes at 2pm ET, and a handful of earnings reports after the close ( APLD and STZ ).
• How we’re thinking about markets - notwithstanding his increasingly bellicose rhetoric, none of the escalatory options available to Trump (civilian infrastructure bombing campaign, militarily reopening Hormuz, and/or seizing the enriched uranium) are good ones and given the huge costs involved in each (along with the fact that the US strategic objectives have been achieved ) , he will be forced to pursue an offramp of some sort . Whether an actual ceasefire deal is achieved remains to be seen, but a significant escalation (especially one involving thousands of US boots on the ground) still seems unlikely , which means the SPX risks are to the upside ( although since 6900-7000 was a firm ceiling before the war, that will be an increasingly difficult threshold to surpass. Even if the fighting fully ends tonight, there is a significant stagflationary burst cascading through the global economy from the last ~5 weeks, the effects of which will be felt for many, many months to come).
• Market in a Minute (macro) – hopes are fading for a deal to be reached between the US and Iran by tonight’s deadline ( WSJ ); Iran submitted a 10-point deal proposal to Washington, demanding a guarantee not to be attacked again, an end to the Lebanon war, and a termination of sanctions ( Trump called the Iranian blueprint a “significant step”, but “not good enough” ) ( NYT ); mediators from Pakistan, Egypt, and Turkey are working to broker an agreement (if Trump sees a deal coming together, he will likely push out the 4/7 8pm ET deadline ) ( Axios ); Pentagon is refining a list of civilian infrastructure targets for Trump to mitigate war crime charges should the White House proceed with a bombing campaign ( Politico ); Iran calls for human chains to be formed around power plants as the US civilian infrastructure deadline nears ( AP ); Iran’s Supreme Leader is said to be unconscious and receiving medical treatment , raising questions about who is actually running the country ( London Times ); China looks to wean its hog industry off American soy to save costs and secure food security ( Reuters )
• Market in a Minute (micro – Tuesday morning) - AAPL (Apple) is encountering engineering and production setbacks for its new foldable i Phone, and this could lead to delays in the launch timeline ( Nikkei ); ASML shares tumble in EU trading after a bill was introduced Thurs night in the US that would ratchet up restrictions on semi equipment sales to China ( Reuters ); Space X aims to allocate a large chunk of its IPO for retail investors, and will host them for an analyst meeting on June 11 ahead of the offering ( Reuters ); Universal Music Group received a takeout bid from Bill Ackman worth more than $63 B, with the stock listing set to shift to NY upon completion of the transaction (under the terms of the deal, UMG would merge with Pershing Square Sparc Holdings, with shareholders receiving EU30.40/shr., including EU5.05/shr. in cash and 0.77 shares in the new entity) ( FT )
• Market in a Minute (micro – Monday night) – AVGO (Broadcom) deals point to upside risk on AI chip sales while Anthropic highlights ARR acceleration ( Vital Knowledge ); CASY (Casey’s General Stores) will replace HOLX (Hologic) in the S&P 500 prior to the opening of trading on Thurs 4/9; managed care stocks spike on updated Medicare rate proposal from CMS ( Vital Knowledge ); PSX (Phillips 66) said it would book a $900 MM mark-to-market loss on hedges due to the sharp recent rise in commodity prices; Samsung Electronics reported blow-out preliminary Q1 results ( Vital Knowledge )
• Calendar for Tuesday 4/7 – the focus will be on US durable/capital goods orders for Feb (8:30am ET), NY Fed consumer expectations survey for Mar (11am ET), US consumer credit for Feb (3pm ET), earnings after the US close ( GBX, LEVI ), analyst meetings ( NTNX ), Japan’s real cash earnings for Feb (Tues night), and New Zealand’s rate decision (Tues night).
Iran

• What bulls are saying : the escalatory options facing Trump are so bad (destroying Iran’s civilian infrastructure, militarily reopening Hormuz, and/or seizing the country’s enriched uranium) that none of them are likely , especially since the bulk of American strategic objectives have been achieved (the administration will be forced to seek out exit ramps lest it preside over a quagmire ). Hormuz isn’t completely shut , as multiple countries strike deals with Iran to ensure safe passage through the waterway ( Iraq was granted permission over the weekend , a significant development since that country was shipping more than 3 M BPD via Hormuz before the war). Trump sounded somewhat hopeful on Monday about the state of negotiations with Iran and expressed optimism that a deal can be reached before the deadline on Tues at 8pm ET. Iran submitted a 10-point deal proposal to Washington, demanding a guarantee not to be attacked again, an end to the Lebanon war, and a termination of sanctions (this was discussed in a NYT report ). According to Axios , mediators from Pakistan, Egypt, and Turkey are working to broker an agreement (if Trump sees a deal coming together, he will likely push out the 4/7 8pm ET deadline ).
• What bears are saying : the situation in Iran remains on the cusp of catastrophe as all the escalatory options available to Trump risk embroiling the Pentagon in a protracted ground campaign involving tens of thousands of troops and/or triggering a (further) massive economic fallout , with Tehran likely to intensify its campaign of terror against critical assets and infrastructure in the Middle East. Trump sounds very serious about launching an aggressive air campaign aimed at destroying Iran’s civil infrastructure , and media reports (including a WSJ article on Monday) continue to suggest this is a very real possibility . Iran rejected the idea of a 45-day ceasefire and demanded a permanent end to the war . According to a WSJ report Mon night, hopes are fading for a deal to be reached by the Tues night deadline . Deescalating is easier said than done with Iran’s ability and willingness to retaliate throughout the region proving more durable than anticipated while Israel’s war against Hezbollah in Lebanon intensifies and the Houthis become more involved (there are also questions about Iran’s remaining missile stockpiles – some reports suggest its arsenal and launching infrastructure remain substantial ). Even if the war completely ends tomorrow, the global economy will still be forced to grapple with a powerful and extended pulse of stagflationary pressure triggered by the multi-week stretch of fighting. Hormuz is never going back to the way it was before – a significant risk premium will be applied to all commodities and goods flowing through the waterway .
• Russia is providing intelligence and cyber assistance to Iran ( Reuters )
US macro

• The services ISM for Mar (out Mon morning) was generally downbeat (and stagflationary) , with the headline index falling 2.1 points M/M to 54 (the Street was modeling 54.9) while many of the key sub-indices painted a gloomy portrait of growth ( business activity/production -6 points M/M, employment -6.6 points, backlog of orders -2.3 points, and new export orders -6.5 points) along with a scorching hot picture of inflation (the prices index surged 7.7 points to 70.7, the highest reading since Oct 2022).
• The services PMI for Mar (out Fri morning) was very weak , signaling that the US economy is “ buckling under the strain of rising prices and intensifying uncertainty ”. The US jobs report (out Fri morning) contained some red flags , including a continued dependence on healthcare for driving employment creation and negative Household Survey details (the number of employed people fell by 64 K, the participation rate dipped, and the civilian labor force size slumped by nearly 400 K people).
• The Fed is likely to be on the sidelines for (at least) the balance of the year when it comes to the Funds Rate, and the pace of asset purchases is set to fall sharply starting next month.
• Fed’s Hammack and Goolsbee warn that inflation is a much larger problem than employment ( Reuters )
• The NY Fed’s Global Supply Chain Pressure Index (which can be found here ) rose to the highest level in more than three years due to disruptions in the Middle East (although it is still well off its COVID highs).
International macro

• Australia’s final services PMI for Mar came in at 46.3, down from the flash reading of 46.6 (Bloomberg)
• China looks to wean its hog industry off American soy to save costs and secure food security ( Reuters )
• ECB official warns of rising inflation risks in the Eurozone ( Reuters )
• Europe’s final services PMI for Mar came in at 50.2, up from the flash reading of 50.1 (Bloomberg)
• Japan household spending came in weaker than expected at -1.8% Y/Y (vs. the Street -0.8%) ( Bloomberg )
• Philippines CPI for Mar ran ahead of the Street at +4.1% (vs. the Street +3.8%) (Bloomberg)
• Sweden’s CPI cools more than anticipated in Mar, bolstering calls for the central bank to extend its rate pause ( Bloomberg )
• Taiwan’s opposition leader is set to arrive in China on Tues for what’s being billed as an “historic journey for peace” ( FT )
• Thailand’s CPI comes in a bit cooler than anticipated at +0.57% core for Mar (vs. the Street 0.7%) (Bloomberg)
• White House labeled erratic by erstwhile US allies around the world , with many wondering if they are even still partners w/America ( WSJ )
Consumer

• CASY (Casey’s General Stores) will replace HOLX (Hologic) in the S&P 500 prior to the opening of trading on Thurs 4/9 .
Energy

• Fuel crisis facing the world right now is much more serious than prior ones according to the head of the IEA ( NBC News )
• PSX (Phillips 66) said it would book a $900 MM mark-to-market loss on hedges due to the sharp recent rise in commodity prices.
• Saudi Arabia will charge a record premium to buyers in Asia given Iran-linked disruptions (buyers in Asia will need to pay ~$20 on top of the benchmark) ( FT )
Financials

• BEN (Franklin) reported month-end AUM for Mar of $1.68 T, down from $1.73 T in Feb, as market declines more than offset net inflows (BEN saw long-term net inflows of $5 B, inclusive of $1 B of long-term net outflows at Western Asset Management).
• IVZ (Invesco) shares slumped on Monday after Black Rock filed paperwork to create its own version of the Nasdaq 100 index (which would create competition for IVZ’s QQQ)
• JPM (JPMorgan) received approval to build one of the largest office towers in Europe ( FT )
• Kalshi can’t be blocked by NJ gaming regulators from offering prediction market services to citizens in the state according to an appeals court decision on Monday ( Reuters )
Healthcare

• Managed care stocks spiked in after-hours trading on Monday after CMS published final 2027 Medicare rates , and the number (+2.48%, or >$13 B in payment increases) was far higher than the preliminary increase proposal from back in Jan (+0.09% or >$700 MM), and the +2.48% is even better than it looks (“when accounting for estimated risk score trend in MA due to factors such as population changes and coding practices , this amounts to a 4.98% increase ”).
M&A/Strategic Actions

• Universal Music Group received a takeout bid from Bill Ackman worth more than $63 B , with the stock listing set to shift to NY upon completion of the transaction (under the terms of the deal, UMG would merge with Pershing Square Sparc Holdings, with shareholders receiving EU30.40/shr. , including EU5.05/shr. in cash and 0.77 shares in the new entity) ( FT )
TMT

• AAPL (Apple) is encountering engineering and production setbacks for its new foldable i Phone, and this could lead to delays in the launch timeline ( Nikkei )
• AI model theft: Open AI, Anthropic , and Google will work together to prevent “adversarial distillation attempts” ( Bloomberg )
• AI is causing “code overload” whereby companies are drowning in a sea of surging lines of code ( NYT )
• AMZN (Amazon)/USPS – Amazon struck a delivery deal w/USPS that will see the postal service retain ~80% of its existing volume with the e-commerce giant, or >1 B packages annually ( Reuters )
• Anthropic said that demand from Claude customers has accelerated so far in 2026 , with ARR now north of $30 B (up from $9 B at the end of 2025). Back in February, Anthropic noted that more than 500 business customers were each spending $1 M in annualized revenue – that number is now above 1,000 .
• ASML shares tumble in EU trading after a bill was introduced Thurs night in the US that would ratchet up restrictions on semi equipment sales to China ( Reuters )
• AVGO (Broadcom) announced a long-term agreement to develop and support custom TPUs for Google , along with supplying networking and other components for Google’s AI racks , through 2031 (in addition, Google, Broadcom, and Anthropic expanded their strategic collaboration under which Anthropic, starting in 2027, will access approximately 3.5 GW of TPU-based compute capacity ). Bottom Line : these agreements point to upside risk to Broadcom’s prior guidance for >$100 B in 2027 AI revenue.
• Music & AI : the major labels have yet to reach a licensing deal w/AI music startup Suno , and talks have hit a stalemate ( FT )
• Open AI’s Sam Altman told Axios that AI superintelligence is “so close” and “so mind-bending” , and will be “so disruptive” ( Axios )
• Open AI’s Sam Altman was the subject of a long profile article in The New Yorker, and the tone of the report was negative, raising questions about his character and ethics ( The New Yorker )
• Samsung Electronics reported blow-out preliminary Q1 results , including sales +68% Y/Y to KRW133 T (vs. the Street KRW117 T) and op. profit up ~750% to KRW57.2 T (vs. the Street KRW39.3 T). Samsung doesn’t provide any detail on segment performance (that will come later in the month alongside the official Q1 release), but it’s very likely memory played a big role in the massive Y/Y increases for both revenue and (especially) profitability ( Vital Knowledge )
• Space X aims to allocate a large chunk of its IPO for retail investors , and will host them for an analyst meeting on June 11 ahead of the offering ( Reuters )
• Universal Music Group received a takeout bid from Bill Ackman worth more than $63 B , with the stock listing set to shift to NY upon completion of the transaction (under the terms of the deal, UMG would merge with Pershing Square Sparc Holdings, with shareholders receiving EU30.40/shr. , including EU5.05/shr. in cash and 0.77 shares in the new entity) ( FT )

Calendar for the week of Monday April 6

• Calendar for Wednesday 4/8 – the focus will be on India’s repurchase rate decision (12:30am ET), German factory orders for Feb (2am ET), Taiwan’s CPI for Mar (4am ET), the Eurozone PPI and retail sales for Feb (5am ET), earnings before the US open ( DAL, RPM ), FOMC minutes from the Mar 18 meeting (2pm ET), and earnings after the US close ( APLD, PSMT, STZ ).
• Calendar for Thursday 4/9 – the focus will be on German exports and industrial production for Feb (2am ET), earnings before the US open ( SMPL ), US personal income/spending for Feb (8:30am ET), the US PCE for Feb (8:30am ET), the US GDP revisions for Q4 (8:30am ET ), US wholesale inventories/trade sales for Feb (10am ET), earnings after the US close ( WDFC ), Japan’s PPI for Mar (Thurs night), China’s PPI/CPI for Mar (Thurs night), and the Bank of Korea policy decision (Thurs night).
• Calendar for Friday 4/10 – the focus will be on Taiwan’s imports/exports for Mar (4am ET), the US CPI for Mar (8:30am ET), US factory orders for Feb (10am ET), and the Michigan sentiment survey for Apr (10am ET).

Calendar for the week of Monday April 13

• What to watch during the week of Monday 4/13 – the main macro events this week include China’s imports/exports for Mar (Mon night), US import/export prices for Mar (Wed morning), the Fed’s Beige Book (Wed afternoon), China’s retail sales/industrial production for Mar (Wed night), and Kevin Warsh’s confirmation hearing before the Senate Banking Committee (Thurs). For US earnings , the key reports include: Mon morning ( GS ); Tues morning ( BLK, C, JNJ, JPM, KMX , and WFC ); Wed morning ( BAC, PNC , and MS ); Wed night ( SLG ); Thurs morning ( ABT, BK, CFG, KEY, MRSH, PEP, SCHW, TRV , and USB ); Thurs night ( AA and NFLX ); and Fri morning ( RF, STT , and TFC ). In Europe, the key earnings reports include: Tues night ( Kering ); Wed morning ( ASML and Hermes ); Thurs morning ( Entain and Pernod Ricard ); and Fri morning ( Ericsson ).
• Calendar for Monday 4/13 – the focus will be on India’s CPI for Mar (6:30am ET), earnings before the US open ( FAST, GS ), US existing home sales for Mar (10am ET), and China’s imports/exports for Mar (Mon night).
• Calendar for Tuesday 4/14 – the focus will be on Japan’s industrial production for Feb (12:30am ET), earnings before the US open ( BLK, C, JNJ, JPM, KMX, WFC ), the US PPI for Mar (8:30am ET), earnings after the EU close ( Kering ), and South Korea’s import/export prices and unemployment rate for Mar (Tues night).
• Calendar for Wednesday 4/15 – the focus will be on earnings before the EU open ( Antofagasta, ASML, Hermes ), EU industrial production for Feb (5am ET), earnings before the US open ( BAC, PGR, PNC, MS, MTB ), the US Empire Manufacturing Index for Apr (8:30am ET), US import/export prices for Mar (8:30am ET), the NAHB housing market index for Apr (10am ET), the Fed’s Beige Book (2pm ET), earnings after the US close ( HOMB, SLG ), China’s new home prices for Mar (Wed night), Australia’s jobs report for Mar (Wed night), and China’s retail sales/industrial production for Mar and GDP for Q1 (Wed night).
• Calendar for Thursday 4/16 – the focus will be on earnings before the EU open ( Entain, Pernod Ricard, Rentokil, Tesco earnings ), UK GDP and industrial/manufacturing production for Feb (2am ET), earnings before the US open ( ABT, BK, CFG, KEY, MRSH, PEP, PLD, SCHW, TRV, USB ), US weekly jobless claims (8:30am ET), the Philadelphia Fed index for April (8:30am ET), US industrial/manufacturing production for March (9:15am ET), and earnings after the US close ( AA, NFLX ).
• Calendar for Friday 4/17 – the focus will be on earnings before the EU open ( Ericsson ), the Eurozone trade balance for Feb (5am ET), and earnings before the US open ( ALLY, ALV, FITB, RF, STT, TFC ).

UNCLE SAM ALTMAN

Open AI’s latest release is a social contract revamp

Morning Brew Design, Photo: Anna Moneymaker/Getty Images

Open AI is handing the government a cheatsheet on how to solve the problems its product might cause. Yesterday, the company released a policy blueprint to address the disruptions it expects impending AI superintelligence to bring—with tips ranging from embracing a four-day workweek to strategies on combating killer robots.
Open AI’s policy menu comes as the public grows increasingly concerned about AI’s potential to cause economic chaos, with some asking the government to step in. The company is also facing questions about CEO Sam Altman’s character , given the potential disruption its product could cause.
New Deal 2.0
Sounding more like a union head, the $852 billion company says AI-driven gains should be shared with workers, with a big role for the government to play.
Here’s what Open AI envisions:

• The government could incentivize companies benefiting from AI to institute a four-day workweek and give employees expanded healthcare and childcare coverage, as well as larger retirement benefits. Government incentives could also help workers displaced by AI switch to industries that rely on human connection like healthcare and education.
• Workers could be taxed less, and programs like Social Security and SNAP would be funded by increased taxes on companies benefiting from the AI boom.
• A public wealth fund could redistribute returns from AI-related investments to citizens, akin to cash payments from Alaska’s oil wealth fund.
• The government could facilitate the expansion of electrical infrastructure powering AI data centers in order to lower household electrical bills. AI companies have warned… that their newest models could enable large-scale cyberattacks and engineered biological weapons in the near future. So, Open AI also shared ways to mitigate AI threats, like giving the government a bigger role in managing risks and expanding international AI-safety cooperation.
Big picture: The Trump administration prefers a light-touch approach to regulating AI, but some in Congress advocate for more proactive measures. Bills to tax AI and mitigate job market impacts are reportedly in the works in the Senate, while Democratic Sen. Bernie Sanders and Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez recently introduced a bill that would put a moratorium on AI data center construction. —SK

Presented By Fisher Investments

Figure out when you can retire

What’s the price of a comfortable retirement? From living expenses to taxes, understanding what your retirement will cost can be tough. Fisher Investments’ guide is here to help you understand the full picture and pave the best way forward to enjoy the future you deserve.

Packed with insights on investing for retirement and useful information on the potential impact of withdrawals on your portfolio, When to Retire: A Quick and Easy Planning Guide can help you reach your goals.

Investors with $1 million, get your free guide today .

WORLD

Tour de headlines

Tom Williams/Getty Images

Trump threatens that Iran “can be taken out in one night.” With the deadline that he imposed of 8pm ET today for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz looming, President Trump said yesterday that “every bridge” and “every power plant” in the country could be attacked in a matter of hours and incapacitated by midnight tonight. The UN warned that attacking Iran’s civilian infrastructure could violate international law, although the president said he was not worried the attacks would be considered war crimes. Trump’s remarks came after Iran reportedly passed on the US’ offer of a 45-day ceasefire to reopen the strait, pushing instead for a permanent end to the war.
Jamie Dimon sees risks ahead. In his annual letter to shareholders, published yesterday, the JPMorgan CEO laid out the potential trouble he sees for the bank and the economy, including geopolitical conflict like the war in Iran, inflation, trouble in private markets, and bank regulations he called “poor.” He warned that inflation slowly creeping up rather than going down could be “the skunk at the party” this year—pushing interest rates up and asset prices down.
Michigan won its first men’s national basketball title since 1989. With a 69-63 victory over UConn in last night’s men’s NCAA championship, the Wolverines snapped a 25-year title drought for the Big Ten. The win also dealt UConn its first loss in a national championship game, with the Huskies falling just short of their third national title in four seasons. With March Madness now over, you’ll have to find something new to banter with your coworkers about. —AR

OY VEY

Festival sponsors drop out after Ye joins lineup

Retamal/Getty Images

Call these brands a 2010 Kanye West single, because they’ve run away: Paypal, Rockstar Energy Drink, and Budweiser-maker AB-In Bev each reportedly said yesterday that they are pulling back from a major UK music festival that recently named Ye as its headliner, becoming the latest brands to abandon ship.
ICYMI: The backlash was swift after London’s Wireless Festival announced last week that Ye, who has a long history of expressing antisemitic views and released a song called “Heil Hitler” less than a year ago, would headline its July event:

• Pepsi withdrew its sponsorship on Sunday after 10 years as the festival’s main brand partner (the official name is “Pepsi MAX Presents Wireless”).
• Liquor giant Diageo bailed shortly thereafter.
• Hours earlier, UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer condemned Ye’s booking as “deeply concerning.” Now, AB-In Bev and Rockstar are also out, per the Wall Street Journal and Variety, respectively. Pay Pal, which hosted a ticket pre-sale last week as a Wireless payment partner, said it won’t let the festival use its branding in promotional materials going forward.
UK ministers are also reviewing Ye’s permission to enter the country.
Meanwhile… Ye just made $33 million selling out two shows at So Fi Stadium in Los Angeles, per Bloomberg, and his new album debuted at No. 2 on the Billboard 200. In January, he placed a full-page ad in the Wall Street Journal apologizing for his past remarks, which he attributed to bipolar disorder. —ML

Together With Turbo Tax

It’s go time. Tax season is here, bringing confusion over new tax laws for entrepreneurs. For the 5.6m+ LLCs formed in 2025 , Turbo Tax Experts for Business can help. They match self-employed and small business owners with a tax expert—so you can file with confidence + save $$$ .

EYES UP HERE

More restaurants, bars are banning phones

Niv Bavarsky

The next time you’re out to dinner, someone might not only ask to take your jacket, they may want you to hand over your phone, too. The number of bars and restaurants establishing a phone-free environment is growing, per Axios—a change that appeals to younger patrons.
Axios found that at least 11 states have individual restaurants or bars with a form of phone restriction or digital detox. Scrolling a menu instead of your phone is thought to create a more intimate setting, lead to more focus on food, and protect patron privacy.
A recent survey from Talker Research shows a significant number of people are putting their phones away and probably don’t want to see someone taking pictures of their food:

• 63% of Gen Z says they intentionally disconnect; 57% of millennials say the same.
• Even older crowds are on board—42% of Gen X and 29% of boomers said they unplug. A needed break: Data from Consumer Affairs showed Americans spend an average of 4.5 hours per day on their devices. Another revealed that 86.5% of phone use involves social networking and texting during meals.
It’s chains, too: The upscale supper club Delilah’s has a no-phones policy. Even some Chick-Fil-A locations are offering free ice cream as an incentive for turning over your phone while eating. —DL

READER POLL

Should phones be allowed in restaurants?

Yes
No
Sometimes, but step outside to take your call

STAT

Prime number: Going the distance

NASA

Here’s something to one up your friend whose pics are always geotagged in exotic locales: Yesterday, the crew of the Artemis II set the record for the farthest humans have ever traveled from Earth (watch the live video here ).

• At 1:57pm ET, the four astronauts flew 252,752 miles from Earth, surpassing the previous record set by the Apollo 13 crew in 1970 by ~4,102 miles.
• “We most importantly choose this moment to challenge this generation and the next to make sure this record is not long-lived,” Canadian astronaut Jeremy Hansen said from the ship. The crew then proceeded to complete a flyby of the moon—including a planned 40 minutes without communication with Earth as they passed the moon’s far side—reaching their maximum distance from Earth of 252,756 miles just after 7pm ET. The crew, who are slated to splash down back on Earth on Friday, did take photos as they traveled, but mostly for science and not for the ’gram. —AR

subject: Final countdown?
body:
What matters in U.S. and global markets today

Morning Bid U.S.

Morning Bid U.S.

A Reuters Open Interest newsletter

What matters in U.S. and global markets today

By Anna Szymanski , Editor-in-Charge, Reuters Open Interest

Markets are once again held in suspense as traders count down to President Donald Trump’s latest deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, with global stocks mixed and oil prices choppy.

Tehran, for its part, has refused to reopen the vital waterway and accept a ceasefire deal, despite the U.S. president’s threats that Iran could be “taken out in one night”, leaving investors braced for the possibility that Trump's latest combative warning could harden into action – or, on the other hand, lead to yet another deadline.

I’ll get into that and more below.

But first, listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

Data refreshes every time you open this email. For more U.S. market news, click here . Please send any feedback to morningbid@thomsonreuters.com .

Today's Market Minute

•

Iran and Israel traded attacks on Tuesday as Tehran refused to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and accept a ceasefire deal ahead of a deadline set by President Trump to agree to his demands or get "taken out" .
•

Space X outlined details of its highly anticipated IPO at a meeting with ‌its team of bankers on Monday night, telling them it plans to earmark a large portion of shares for retail investors .
•

Samsung Electronics on Tuesday projected its first-quarter earnings would exceed its entire profit for last year, beating expectations as booming demand for ‌AI infrastructure stretched supply and drove chip prices higher .

• World governments should have no delusions about the Iran war: even with a ceasefire deal or a reopened strait, an energy crisis is locked in, argues ROI Asia Commodities Columnist Clyde Russell .

•

The Middle East energy crisis looks like the kind of shock that central banks should “look through” - but policymakers may not be able to sit on their hands for long, whether that’s the right choice or not, writes former Reuters Senior Editor Mike Peacock .

Final countdown?

As much of the financial world returned from the long holiday weekend on Tuesday, investors appeared to be in a wait-and-see mode with Trump’s latest deadline for Iran – 8 p.m. EDT tonight – drawing closer.

Oil prices moved around early on Tuesday, with Brent crude initially rising to over $111 per barrel before paring those gains. U.S. WTI crude is hovering around $113/bbl after briefly topping $116.

Equities also got off to a cautious start , with major Asian indexes largely flat, despite a lift in the mood early in the session after a record-breaking quarterly profit forecast from Samsung Electronics . European shares rose after the open.

Major U.S. indexes rose on Monday , partly because traders were heartened by reports of continued discussions about a potential ceasefire. While that optimism faded after Tehran rejected a short-term truce, mediation by Pakistan is reportedly continuing . U.S. stock futures were roughly flat before the bell.

The greenback dipped slightly but remained broadly firm , with the dollar index hovering just under the 100 level after hitting its highest level since May 2025 last week. The yen, on the other hand, continues to languish near the 160-per-dollar level.

Recent macro signals from the U.S. are adding a layer of complexity as traders mull the wider impacts of the Iran energy shock.

The latest survey results from the Institute for Supply Management, released on Monday, showed that U.S. services sector growth slowed in March while prices paid by businesses for inputs rose by the most in more than 13 years – an early sign of rising inflation pressures amid the war. For more on that front, we’ll have to wait for U.S. March CPI inflation figures set for release this Friday.

No matter what that release shows, the global economic outlook appears to be darkening, with the IMF warning on Monday that “all roads” lead to higher prices and slower growth, according to remarks made by its head, Kristalina Georgieva.

Ultimately, the big question – yet again – is whether Trump’s latest deadline will result in the conflict being ramped up or down. We’ll all have to wait and see.

Today's key chart

Graphics are produced by Reuters.

The world's largest memory chipmaker today announced an estimated operating profit of 57.2 trillion won ($37.92 billion) for the January to March period, compared with an LSEG Smart Estimate of 40.6 trillion won and a more than eightfold jump from 6.69 trillion won a year earlier.

And, TSA tips fuel over 800 ICE arrests.

Daily Briefing

Daily Briefing

By Anisha De

Hello. Iran defiant on eve of Trump's ceasefire deadline , tips from TSA fuel more ICE arrests than previously known and Space X lays out IPO details .

Plus, pictures of Earth and the moon from the Artemis II historic voyage.

Today's Top News

The rubble of a building of the Sharif University of Technology, damaged in a strike in Tehran, Iran, April 7, 2026. Majid Asgaripour/WANA (West Asia News Agency) via REUTERS

War in the Middle East

•

Iran and Israel traded attacks as Tehran refused to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and accept a ceasefire deal on the eve of a deadline set by US President Donald Trump ‌to agree to his demands or get "taken out."
•

Russian satellites have made dozens of detailed imagery surveys of military facilities and critical sites across the Middle East to help Iran strike US forces and other targets, according to a Ukrainian intelligence assessment. Read our exclusive report .
•

At least two attackers were killed and one was seriously injured in an extended gun ‌battle between police and assailants directly outside the building housing the Israeli consulate in Istanbul, according to media reports and Reuters video.

In other news

•

US Immigration and Customs Enforcement arrested more than 800 people following tips shared by federal airport security officials from the start of Trump's presidency through February 2026, internal ICE data reviewed by ‌Reuters show, a figure far above what was previously publicly known.
•

The White House is proposing to cut more than 9,400 workers and just over $1.5 billion from the 60,000-employee Transportation ‌Security Administration that handles airport security operations, according to budget documents.
•

Specialist mini jet engine makers across Europe are ramping up production and investment to head off a looming supply shortage that threatens to hold back Ukraine's rapid deep-strike drone programme at a critical stage of the war with Russia.
•

US Vice President JD Vance is traveling to Hungary on a mission to boost the campaign of nationalist Prime Minister Viktor Orban, who faces the toughest re-election bid of his career. Correspondent Anita Komuves joins the Reuters World News podcas t to explain why some global leaders are lining up behind him.

• Resident doctors in England started a six-day walkout after rejecting an offer the government said would not get better, with the British Medical Association saying it failed to reverse years of pay erosion and staffing pressures.
•

Australia's most decorated soldier, Ben Roberts-Smith, was arrested and charged with five counts of war crimes relating to the killing of unarmed civilians while on deployment in Afghanistan.

Business & Markets

Space X's logo and an Elon Musk photo are seen in this illustration created on December 19, 2022. REUTERS/Dado Ruvic/Illustration/File Photo

•

Space X outlined details of its highly anticipated IPO at a meeting with ‌its team of bankers, telling them it plans to earmark a large portion of shares for retail investors and will host 1,500 of them at an event in June following the IPO roadshow launch, according to two people familiar with the matter. However, the blockbuster listing could suck the oxygen out of the fragile US IPO market .

• Bill Ackman's Pershing Square proposed merging its acquisition vehicle ‌with Universal Music Group with a plan to list in the United States in a deal aimed at reviving the world's biggest music label's value.
•

Americans starting weight-loss medicines for the first time want lower cost and greater convenience as they consider pills from Novo Nordisk or Eli Lilly according to seven doctors who specialize in obesity. Read how the pill vs. shot debate is reshaping the weight loss drug market.
•

Air India CEO Campbell Wilson has resigned , a source with direct knowledge of ‌the matter said, as the airline grapples with persistent losses and heightened regulatory scrutiny following a crash last year that killed 260 people.
•

Brazil has put China's BYD on a ‌registry of employers who have subjected workers to conditions similar to slavery , after a 2024 scandal in which Chinese workers were said to have been victims of human trafficking and abusive contracts.
•

Samsung Electronics projected its first-quarter earnings would exceed its entire profit for last year , beating expectations as booming demand for artificial intelligence infrastructure stretched supply and drove chip prices higher.

Chinese pigs fed new menu as Beijing weans farmers off US soy

Farm manager Gao Qinshan feeds pigs in a pig pen at a farm in Taizhou, Jiangsu province, China January 15, 2026. REUTERS/Go Nakamura/File Photo

At the edge of one of the many pig farms spread across the vast, unbroken floodplains of Taizhou, a two-hour drive northwest of Shanghai, a pair of square, four-meter pools of acrid-smelling ochre liquid hold the key to cutting costly soybean use in half.

The pools hold a swill of cheaper, locally sourced ingredients, which ‌can include brans, pumpkin vines and wine lees. But it is fermented - like yogurt - so the proteins are already broken down and easy to digest, lessening the need for the higher-quality proteins in soy, 80% of which China imports.
The grassroots fixation on overheads belies Beijing's more strategic motivations: long‑term food security and increased self‑reliance.

Read our story

And Finally...

NASA astronaut and Artemis II mission specialist Christina Koch peers out of one of the Orion spacecraft's main cabin windows, April 2, 2026. NASA/Handout via REUTERS/File Photo

The four astronauts of NASA's Artemis II mission flew deeper into space than any humans before them , as they cruised through a rare flyby of the shadowed far side of the moon that revealed a lunar surface under cosmic bombardment.
Here's some of the stunning views of the Earth and the moon from the historic voyage.

Tuesday - April 07, 2026 |

Presented by Alumni Ventures

Market Overview

S&P 500

Stock & Market News

•
Space X IPO targets June roadshow: Space X has outlined plans for a June IPO roadshow, aiming to raise $75 B at up to a $1.75 T valuation, with an unusually large retail allocation that could reshape IPO participation. Source: Reuters .

•
Medicare rates boost insurers: The U.S. is raising Medicare Advantage payments by 2.48% for 2027, adding ~$13 B and lifting insurers like United Health ( UNH ), Elevance ( ELV ), Humana ( HUM ), and CVS Health ( CVS ) as investors see improved policy support. Source: WSJ .

•
Amazon and USPS reach deal: Amazon ( AMZN ) and the U.S. Postal Service agreed to a new delivery contract cutting Amazon’s package volume by 20%. The move eases prior plans for deeper cuts but still risking USPS revenue tied to roughly $6 B annually from Amazon shipments. Source: WSJ .

•
Ackman bids for UMG: Pershing Square ( LON:PSH ) has proposed a $64 B takeover of Universal Music ( AMS:UMG ) with a 78% premium and a U.S. relisting plan, sending shares up 9%. Source: CNBC .

•
ASML shares fall on curbs: ASML ( ASML ) fell 1% premarket after U.S. lawmakers proposed new export restrictions targeting DUV chip tools to China. Source: CNBC .

•
Apple foldable faces delays: Apple Inc. ( AAPL ) is encountering engineering issues with its first foldable i Phone that could delay mass production and shipments by months, potentially pushing back its planned 2026 launch. Source: Reuters .

•
Deere settles repair lawsuit: Deere & Company ( DE ) has agreed to a $99 M settlement and a 10-year commitment to provide farmers with repair tools and diagnostics, addressing right-to-repair claims while denying wrongdoing. Source: Reuters .

•
Netflix launches kids' games app: Netflix ( NFLX ) is launching its “Playground” app for kids, expanding its gaming push with ad-free titles tied to popular shows as it refocuses strategy on family content and cloud-based gaming growth. Source: The Verge .

•
Mellon, Robinhood form Trump account pact: Bank of New York Mellon ( BK ) and Robinhood ( HOOD ) are partnering with the Department of the Treasury to launch a new app for “Trump accounts,” tax-advantaged investment accounts for children that include a $1,000 government contribution. Source: CNBC .

Sponsored

Exclusive: You Could Be an Investor in High-Potential Startups Like These

This week only, Alumni Ventures is offering readers early access to high-growth startup opportunities , including some of today’s most exciting AI, Deep Tech, Quantum Computing, Cybersecurity, and Space companies co-invested alongside top VC firms like Andreessen Horowitz (a16z), Bessemer, & Y Combinator.

You get:

• Curated deal flow of high-potential startups

• AV is already investing alongside elite lead venture firms in these deals

• No cost to see deals

• No obligation to invest

Don’t miss your chance before access closes.

Join Alumni Ventures Syndicate Today →

AI News

•
Broadcom's AI pacts: Broadcom ( AVGO ) rose 3% premarket after securing AI chip deals with Google ( GOOGL ) and Anthropic , reinforcing long-term demand visibility. Source: CNBC .

•
Anthropic's PE venture: Anthropic is in talks to invest $200 M in a new venture with private equity firms, aiming to help portfolio companies adopt its AI tools. The company has also topped a $30 B revenue run rate and is expanding partnerships with Broadcom ( AVGO ) and Alphabet ( GOOGL ). Sources: WSJ / Bloomberg .

•
Open AI seeks Musk probe: Open AI is urging regulators to investigate Elon Musk over alleged anti-competitive actions tied to its restructuring, escalating a legal battle that also involves Microsoft ( MSFT ) and could impact AI industry competition. Source: Bloomberg .

•
Samsung profit surges on AI: Samsung Electronics ( KRX:005930 ) posted a record 755% profit jump driven by strong AI memory demand, boosting peers like SK hynix ( KRX:000660 ). Source: Bloomberg .

•
AI firms unite vs. China: Open AI , Anthropic , and Google ( GOOGL ) are collaborating to combat unauthorized “distillation” of their AI models, particularly by Chinese firms, which they say threatens revenue and national security. Source: Bloomberg .

•
Open AI proposes AI policy shift: Open AI has released policy proposals, including a public wealth fund, expanded safety nets, and grid investment, to address AI-driven economic disruption. Source: Bloomberg .

Economic News

Photo by David Syphers on Unsplash

•
Oil rises on Iran risk: Oil prices are climbing as tensions around the Strait of Hormuz persist, raising risks to global supply and inflation, with Brent above $111 and U.S. crude near $116. Source: BBC .

•
Asian airlines cut flights: Airlines across Asia are cutting flights, carrying extra fuel, and adding refueling stops as the Strait of Hormuz disruption reduces jet fuel supply and drives prices sharply higher. Source: Reuters .

•
IMF warns of stagflation risk: IMF managing director Kristalina Georgieva said that the Iran war is driving higher inflation and slower growth as oil supply drops 13% and Hormuz disruptions persist, with poorer nations hit hardest and forecasts likely to be downgraded. Source: CNBC .

World News & Politics

•
U.S.-Iran deal hopes fade: Trump’s deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz is approaching with no clear diplomatic breakthrough, raising risks of wider infrastructure attacks and further volatility across energy and global markets. Source: NY Times .

•
Vietnam leader consolidates power: To Lam was elected president of Vietnam while retaining his role as Communist Party chief, consolidating power in a shift away from collective leadership that could enable faster policy decisions but raises authoritarianism concerns. Source: Reuters .

•
Vance backs Orban ahead of vote: U.S. VP JD Vance is visiting Hungary to support Viktor Orban before elections. The U.S. and Russia back Orban, while the EU eyes potential policy shifts. Source: NY Times .

•
Artemis II completes flyby: NASA’s Artemis II crew is returning to Earth after a historic Moon flyby, breaking distance records and advancing data collection to support future space exploration plans. Source: NPR .

•
Trump proposes TSA cuts: The Trump administration is proposing to cut 9,400 TSA jobs and $1.5 B from its budget, a ~20% reduction, potentially impacting airport operations and airlines. Source: Reuters .
